name.title: Source name
name.summary: Name the source after the channels or campaign you are scanning.
suggestions: Reference the brand or channel set (example: Brand channels).
Distinguish multiple YouTube sources by audience or region.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Provide at least one of channels (to list videos) or video_urls (explicit videos).
suggestions: Use channel handles or URLs like @OpenAI or https://www.youtube.com/@OpenAI.
Use video_urls for a fixed set of watch URLs to audit.
You can combine channels and explicit video_urls in one source.
questions: Which channels or specific videos do you want to scan?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: No credentials are needed for public videos; optionally supply cookies.
suggestions: Leave masked empty for public content.
Provide Netscape-format cookies only to reach age-restricted or members-only videos.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.transcript for caption languages and optional.connection for proxy/timeout tuning.
suggestions: Set optional.transcript.languages (example: ["en"]) to prefer specific caption languages.
Enable optional.transcript.skip_transcript for metadata-only scans (no detector content).
Set optional.connection.proxy_url to mitigate rate limiting at scale.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many channel videos are processed per run.
suggestions: Use LATEST with rows_per_page to scan the newest N videos per channel.
Use RANDOM for broad spot checks across a channel.
Use ALL only when a full channel backfill is required.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Name the source after the mailbox or provider so coverage is obvious.
suggestions: Include the account and provider (example: Support Inbox (Gmail)).
If you scan several mailboxes, make each name distinct.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Provide the IMAP server host and port for the mailbox provider.
suggestions: Gmail: imap.gmail.com, port 993.
Outlook / Microsoft 365: outlook.office365.com, port 993.
Most providers use port 993 for IMAP over TLS.
questions: Which email provider or IMAP host are you connecting to?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Authenticate with the mailbox username and an app password (stored masked).
suggestions: Use the full email address as the username.
Generate an app password — Gmail and Outlook block normal passwords over IMAP when 2FA is on.
The password is stored only in masked config.
questions: Do you have an app password for this mailbox?

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.scope to choose folders, date ranges, and attachment handling, and optional.connection for transport tuning.
suggestions: Set optional.scope.folders (default INBOX) to target specific mailboxes.
Use unseen_only or since_date/before_date to limit the message set.
Keep include_attachments on so attachments become linked, separately scanned assets.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many messages are processed per run.
suggestions: Use LATEST for frequent syncs focused on the newest messages.
Use RANDOM for broad spot checks across the mailbox.
Use ALL only when a full historical scan is required.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Pick a short name that makes the workspace obvious to your team.
suggestions: Use the workspace name or domain (example: Acme Slack).
If you have multiple environments, include prod/dev in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Workspace helps stable naming and links, but token auth is what enables ingestion.
suggestions: Set workspace to the short name or domain (example: acme).
You can still ingest without workspace, but URLs and IDs are cleaner when it is set.
questions: What is the Slack workspace name or domain?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Choose the token type based on which channels you need.
suggestions: Bot tokens (xoxb-) are best for public channels.
User tokens (xoxp-) are required for DMs or private channels.
If you use token + token_type, set token_type to bot or user.
questions: Do you need private channels/DMs? If yes, use a user token.
Which token do you want to use?

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.channels, optional.time_range, and optional.ingestion to tune scope and performance.
suggestions: Use channel_types to discover channels; set channel_ids for an explicit list.
Use oldest/latest to limit date range in optional.time_range.
Tune optional.ingestion.batch_size and rate_limit_delay_seconds for API limits.
Enable optional.ingestion.include_thread_replies for full context (more API calls).

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many messages are fetched per channel each run.
suggestions: Use LATEST to get the most recent messages in each channel.
Use RANDOM for a broad cross-channel signal.
Use ALL to ingest every message — only suitable for small or infrequently updated workspaces.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Name it after the site so it is recognizable later.
suggestions: Use the site name or domain (example: Example Blog).
If you manage multiple sites, include the environment name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.url is the only mandatory field for ingestion.
suggestions: Use the full HTTPS URL (example: https://example.com).
If this is a sub-site, include the subdomain or path.
questions: What is the WordPress site URL?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Only needed for private or draft content.
suggestions: Public sites can leave masked empty.
For drafts/private, set masked.username and masked.application_password.
Application passwords are created in the WordPress user profile.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.content controls extraction scope.
suggestions: Toggle optional.content.fetch_posts / fetch_pages to include or exclude content types.
Use optional.content.post_status to include drafts or private posts.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many posts and pages are fetched per run.
suggestions: Use LATEST to always fetch the most recently modified content first.
Use RANDOM for a varied cross-section of your site.
Use ALL to scan the entire site — only recommended for small WordPress installs.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a storage-scoped name that makes endpoint and bucket intent obvious.
suggestions: Include provider and data domain (example: R2 Marketing Exports).
Add environment markers when needed (prod/staging/dev).

required.title: Required
required.summary: Set required.bucket for the S3-compatible container to scan.
suggestions: Use this source for AWS S3, MinIO, Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2, Garage, and other S3-compatible endpoints.
Set optional.connection.endpoint_url for non-AWS providers.
questions: What bucket should be scanned?
Do you need a custom endpoint_url?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Use static access keys or leave masked empty to rely on ambient AWS credential chain.
suggestions: Set aws_access_key_id + aws_secret_access_key (+ aws_session_token for temporary credentials).
For AWS-native environments, IAM role/instance profile can be enough.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.connection and optional.scope to tune S3 listing/downloading behavior.
suggestions: Set optional.scope.prefix to focus on a path-like subset.
Use include_extensions/exclude_extensions to limit formats (for example .pdf, .csv, .parquet).
Tune optional.connection.max_object_bytes to control download cost for content parsing.
Set include_content_preview=false if you only need object metadata indexing.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many objects are listed/processed each run.
suggestions: Use LATEST to prioritize newly modified objects.
Use RANDOM for broad coverage across large containers.
Use ALL for full inventory sweeps when runtime and cost are acceptable.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a container-scoped name that includes account and container intent.
suggestions: Include account and domain (example: Azure Finance Archive).
Add environment markers when needed (prod/staging/dev).

required.title: Required
required.summary: Set required.account_url and required.container for Azure Blob Storage.
suggestions: Use account_url like https://<account>.blob.core.windows.net.
Set container to the exact Azure Blob container name.
questions: What account_url should be scanned?
Which container should be scanned?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Use connection string, account key/SAS, or service principal credentials.
suggestions: Prefer azure_connection_string for quick setup.
Use azure_account_key or azure_sas_token when needed.
Use azure_client_id + azure_client_secret + azure_tenant_id for service principal auth.
Leave masked empty to rely on managed identity/default credential chain.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.connection and optional.scope to tune listing/downloading behavior.
suggestions: Set optional.scope.prefix to focus on a virtual folder prefix.
Use include_extensions/exclude_extensions to limit formats.
Tune optional.connection.max_object_bytes to control preview download cost.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many blobs are listed/processed each run.
suggestions: Use LATEST to prioritize recently modified blobs.
Use RANDOM for broad coverage in large containers.
Use ALL for full inventory sweeps when runtime and cost are acceptable.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a bucket-scoped name that clearly identifies data domain and environment.
suggestions: Include bucket purpose (example: GCS Product Exports).
Add environment markers when needed (prod/staging/dev).

required.title: Required
required.summary: Set required.bucket for the Google Cloud Storage bucket to scan.
suggestions: Use the exact GCS bucket name.
Set optional.connection.project_id when multiple project contexts are possible.
questions: What bucket should be scanned?
Do you need a specific project_id override?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Use inline service account JSON or ambient ADC/workload identity.
suggestions: Set gcp_credentials_json for inline service account credentials.
Use optional.connection.gcp_credentials_file for file-based credentials.
Leave masked empty to rely on GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS or workload identity.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.connection and optional.scope to tune listing/downloading behavior.
suggestions: Set optional.scope.prefix to focus on a key prefix.
Use include_extensions/exclude_extensions to limit formats.
Tune optional.connection.max_object_bytes to control preview download cost.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many GCS objects are listed/processed each run.
suggestions: Use LATEST to prioritize recently modified objects.
Use RANDOM for broad coverage in large buckets.
Use ALL for full inventory sweeps when runtime and cost are acceptable.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a folder-scoped name that makes the scanned path obvious. Desktop-only: this source scans a folder on the machine running the app and is not available in hosted/kubernetes/docker deployments.
suggestions: Include the folder's purpose (example: Local Downloads Folder).
Add environment or machine markers when needed.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Set required.path to the absolute path of the local folder to scan.
suggestions: Only available in the desktop application (or local development) — scans run with the app's own filesystem permissions.
Use an absolute path (example: /Users/jane/Documents).
questions: What absolute folder path should be scanned?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: No credentials are required; scans run with the desktop application's filesystem permissions.
suggestions: Leave masked empty — there is nothing to configure here.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.traversal and optional.scope to tune directory traversal and file filtering.
suggestions: Set optional.traversal.max_depth to limit how deep the scan recurses.
Use include_extensions/exclude_extensions to limit formats (for example .pdf, .csv, .docx).
Enable optional.traversal.include_hidden to include dot-files and hidden directories.
Tune optional.traversal.max_file_bytes to control how much of each file is read for detection.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many files are listed/processed each run.
suggestions: Use LATEST to prioritize recently modified files.
Use RANDOM for broad coverage across large folders.
Use ALL for full inventory sweeps when runtime and cost are acceptable.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a database-oriented name so teams can quickly identify scope.
suggestions: Include environment and system name (example: Billing Postgres Prod).
If this is schema-scoped, include schema in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.host and required.port are mandatory; everything else is configured under optional sections.
suggestions: Set required.host and required.port to DB endpoint values reachable from the runner.
Use optional.connection.ssl_mode/connect_timeout_seconds for extra connection controls.
Use optional.scope for database/schema/table selection.
questions: What host should be scanned?
Do you want a single database or all visible databases?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: masked.username and masked.password are required for standard PostgreSQL auth.
suggestions: Use a least-privilege read-only account.
Verify the account can read information_schema and target tables.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection and optional.scope tune connection behavior and scan scope.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.database for focused scans; include_all_databases for broad coverage.
Use optional.scope.include_schemas/exclude_schemas to control cost.
Use optional.connection.ssl_mode and connect_timeout_seconds for secure or slow hosts.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how rows are sampled from each table.
suggestions: Use LATEST with order_by_column=updated_at to focus on recently changed rows.
Use RANDOM for a broad, unbiased sample across all tables.
Use ALL to read every row — only suitable for small tables or targeted audits.
Use rows_per_page to control sample size for RANDOM/LATEST and pagination batch size for ALL strategy.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a database-oriented name so teams can quickly identify scope.
suggestions: Include environment and system name (example: Billing MySQL Prod).
If this is database-scoped, include database in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.host and required.port are mandatory; everything else is configured under optional sections.
suggestions: Set required.host and required.port to DB endpoint values reachable from the runner.
Use optional.connection.connect_timeout_seconds for extra connection controls.
Use optional.scope for database and table selection.
questions: What host should be scanned?
Do you want a single database or all visible databases?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: masked.username and masked.password are required for standard MySQL auth.
suggestions: Use a least-privilege read-only account.
Verify the account can read information_schema and target tables.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection and optional.scope tune connection behavior and scan scope.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.database for focused scans; include_all_databases for broad coverage.
Use optional.scope.include_tables to target specific tables.
Use optional.connection.connect_timeout_seconds for slow or remote hosts.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how rows are sampled from each table.
suggestions: Use LATEST with order_by_column=updated_at to focus on recently changed rows.
Use RANDOM for a broad, unbiased sample across all tables.
Use ALL to read every row — only suitable for small tables or targeted audits.
Use rows_per_page to control sample size for RANDOM/LATEST and pagination batch size for ALL strategy.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a database-oriented name so teams can quickly identify scope.
suggestions: Include environment and system name (example: Billing MSSQL Prod).
If this is database-scoped, include database in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.host and required.port are mandatory; everything else is configured under optional sections.
suggestions: Set required.host and required.port to SQL Server endpoint values reachable from the runner.
Use optional.connection.connect_timeout_seconds for slow or remote hosts.
Use optional.scope for database, schema, and object selection.
questions: What host should be scanned?
Which SQL Server port should be used?
Do you want a single database or all visible databases?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: masked.username and masked.password are required. Choose optional.connection.auth_mode as CUSTOM or LDAP.
suggestions: Use a least-privilege read-only account.
For LDAP mode, set optional.connection.ldap_domain to auto-prefix the username.
Verify the account can read INFORMATION_SCHEMA and target tables/views.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection and optional.scope tune connection behavior and scan scope.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.database for focused scans; include_all_databases for broad coverage.
Use optional.scope.include_tables/include_views to control whether tables or views are extracted.
Use optional.scope.include_objects for explicit object allowlists.
Set optional.connection.is_aws_rds explicitly in production to avoid environment detection ambiguity.
Use optional.extraction.include_table_lineage/include_view_lineage to control lineage link generation.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how rows are sampled from each SQL Server table/view.
suggestions: Use LATEST with order_by_column=updated_at to focus on recently changed rows.
Use RANDOM for a broad, unbiased sample across tables and views.
Use ALL to read every row — only suitable for small datasets or targeted audits.
Use rows_per_page to control sample size for RANDOM/LATEST and pagination batch size for ALL strategy.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a service-oriented name so teams can quickly identify Oracle scope.
suggestions: Include environment and system name (example: Billing Oracle PROD).
If this is service-scoped, include service name in the source name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.host, required.port, and required.service_name are mandatory; everything else is configured under optional sections.
suggestions: Set required.host and required.port to Oracle endpoint values reachable from the runner.
Set required.service_name to the Oracle service identifier (for example TEST_PDB).
Use optional.connection.connect_timeout_seconds for slow or remote hosts.
questions: What Oracle host should be scanned?
Which Oracle port should be used?
What Oracle service_name should be used?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: masked.username and masked.password are required for standard Oracle auth.
suggestions: Use a least-privilege read-only account.
Verify the account can read ALL_TABLES, ALL_VIEWS, ALL_TAB_COLUMNS, and dependency metadata.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection and optional.scope tune connection behavior, scope, and lineage depth.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.include_tables/include_views to control extracted object types.
Enable optional.scope.include_view_lineage and include_view_column_lineage when downstream relationship context is needed.
Use optional.scope.include_objects for explicit object allowlists.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how rows are sampled from each Oracle table/view.
suggestions: Use LATEST with order_by_column=updated_at to focus on recently changed rows.
Use RANDOM for a broad, unbiased sample across tables and views.
Use ALL to read every row — only suitable for small datasets or targeted audits.
Use rows_per_page to control sample size for RANDOM/LATEST and pagination batch size for ALL strategy.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a warehouse-oriented name so teams can quickly identify Hive scope.
suggestions: Include environment and platform name (example: Analytics Hive Prod).
If this is database-scoped, include the database in the source name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.host and required.port are mandatory; everything else is configured under optional sections.
suggestions: Set required.host and required.port to Hive endpoint values reachable from the runner.
Use optional.scope.database for single-database scans or include_all_databases for broad coverage.
Set optional.connection.scheme when using HTTP/HTTPS transports (for example hive+https).
questions: What Hive host should be scanned?
Which Hive port should be used?
Do you want a single Hive database or all visible databases?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: masked.username and masked.password are used for username/password and LDAP-style Hive authentication.
suggestions: Use a least-privilege read-only account.
For LDAP auth, set optional.connection.connect_args.auth to LDAP.
For Kerberos auth, use optional.connection.connect_args.auth=KERBEROS and set kerberos_service_name when needed.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection and optional.scope tune transport/authentication behavior and scan scope.
suggestions: Use optional.connection.connect_args to pass Hive driver arguments (auth, kerberos_service_name, http_path).
Use optional.scope.include_tables/include_views to control extracted object types.
Use optional.scope.include_objects to target explicit objects.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how rows are sampled from each Hive table/view.
suggestions: Use LATEST with order_by_column=updated_at when tables store recency fields.
Use RANDOM for broad coverage across Hive objects.
Use ALL for complete scans on small datasets or targeted audits.
Use rows_per_page to control sample size for RANDOM/LATEST and pagination batch size for ALL strategy.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a workspace/catalog-oriented name so teams can quickly identify Databricks Unity Catalog scope.
suggestions: Include workspace and domain (example: Finance Databricks Unity Catalog).
If scoped to specific catalogs, include them in the source name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.workspace_url and required.warehouse_id are mandatory. Authentication mode switches between PAT_TOKEN and SERVICE_PRINCIPAL.
suggestions: Use PAT_TOKEN for personal access token auth with masked.token.
Use SERVICE_PRINCIPAL with required.client_id and masked.client_secret for non-user automation.
Set required.warehouse_id to enable SQL-based row sampling.
questions: Which Databricks workspace_url should be scanned?
Which SQL warehouse_id should be used for sampling?
Do you want PAT_TOKEN or SERVICE_PRINCIPAL authentication?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Store Databricks secrets only in masked fields.
suggestions: PAT_TOKEN mode requires masked.token.
SERVICE_PRINCIPAL mode requires masked.client_secret.
Avoid embedding credentials in workspace URLs.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection tunes API/statement behavior, optional.scope narrows Unity Catalog objects, and optional.extraction controls lineage/notebook/pipeline metadata.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.include_catalogs/include_schemas/include_tables for focused scans.
Enable optional.extraction.include_table_lineage to populate table links.
Enable include_notebooks/include_pipelines only when you need those metadata assets.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls sampled rows per Unity Catalog table during detector payload generation.
suggestions: Use LATEST with order_by_column=updated_at for recency-focused monitoring.
Use RANDOM for broad row coverage across many tables.
Use ALL for complete scans only on small scoped datasets.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use an account-oriented name so teams can quickly identify Snowflake scope.
suggestions: Include account and business domain (example: Finance Snowflake Prod).
If this is database-scoped, include the database name in the source name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: required.account_id and required.authentication_type are mandatory. Authentication type switches masked fields.
suggestions: Use DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATOR with masked.username and masked.password for standard service accounts.
Use KEY_PAIR_AUTHENTICATOR with masked.username and masked.private_key for key-pair auth.
Set account_id as locator/org-account form; full host URLs are normalized automatically.
questions: Which Snowflake account_id should be scanned?
Which authentication_type do you want to use?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Store Snowflake credentials and key material only in masked fields.
suggestions: DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATOR requires masked.username and masked.password.
KEY_PAIR_AUTHENTICATOR requires masked.username and masked.private_key (plus optional private_key_password).
OAUTH_AUTHENTICATOR_TOKEN requires masked.username and masked.token.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection tunes account/session settings, optional.scope narrows object selection, and optional.extraction controls lineage windows.
suggestions: Use optional.connection.warehouse and role for predictable SQL context.
Use optional.scope.database/include_schemas/include_objects to constrain scan cost.
Set optional.extraction.start_time with include_table_lineage/include_view_lineage to bound lineage extraction.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how rows are sampled from each Snowflake table/view.
suggestions: Use LATEST with order_by_column=UPDATED_AT when recency fields exist.
Use RANDOM for broad row coverage across scoped objects.
Use ALL only for small schemas or focused audits.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a deployment-oriented name so teams can quickly identify the MongoDB scope.
suggestions: Include environment and deployment type (example: Finance MongoDB Atlas Prod).
If the scan is scoped to a single database, include the database name (example: Chunker MongoDB Atlas — chunker).
For multi-database scans, use the cluster name (example: BIGFREE Atlas — all databases).

required.title: Required
required.summary: Choose deployment mode first — Atlas uses cluster_host, on-prem uses host and port.
suggestions: For Atlas: set required.deployment=ATLAS and required.cluster_host to the bare host (bigfree.zaz2dbb.mongodb.net) or a full mongodb+srv:// URI — credentials in the URI are discarded, always use masked fields.
For on-prem: set required.deployment=ON_PREM with required.host (e.g. mongo.internal) and required.port (default 27017).
Never embed username or password in cluster_host — use masked.username and masked.password instead.
questions: Is this MongoDB Atlas or self-hosted / on-prem?
What is the Atlas cluster host, or the on-prem host and port?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Use masked.username and masked.password for SCRAM authentication. Pass an empty object {} for anonymous/no-auth access.
suggestions: Use a least-privilege read-only account (e.g. a user with the readAnyDatabase role on Atlas).
Set optional.connection.auth_mechanism=SCRAM-SHA-256 for Atlas (recommended) or SCRAM-SHA-1 for older deployments.
Set optional.connection.auth_source=admin when the user account lives in the admin database (common for on-prem).
For unauthenticated local dev clusters, pass masked={} (empty object) — the MongoDBMaskedNone variant.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection tunes authentication and TLS; optional.scope controls which databases and collections are scanned.
suggestions: Scope to a single database: set optional.scope.database='mydb' and optional.scope.include_all_databases=false — faster and cheaper.
Scan all databases: leave include_all_databases=true; system databases (admin, config, local) are excluded by default via optional.scope.exclude_databases.
Use optional.scope.include_collections or exclude_collections (as 'collection' or 'database.collection' strings) to allow-/deny-list specific collections.
Cap cost on wide clusters: set optional.scope.collection_limit (e.g. 50) to stop after N collections per database.
TLS: optional.connection.tls=true is usually required for remote/cloud deployments; Atlas handles it by default.
Replica sets: set optional.connection.replica_set for on-prem replica set names.
Connection timeout: optional.connection.connect_timeout_ms defaults to 10000 ms; increase for slow networks.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many documents are sampled from each collection and in what order.
suggestions: Use RANDOM (default) for broad, unbiased coverage — uses MongoDB $sample aggregation per collection.
Use LATEST to prioritise the newest documents (sorted descending by _id or a custom order_by_column); useful for audit-trail or event collections.
Use ALL only for small, targeted collections — reads every document.
Set limit (default 100) to the number of documents per collection; lower values (10-30) for large clusters, higher (200+) for focused audits.
Set order_by_column to a timestamp field (e.g. updated_at) combined with LATEST to get truly recent documents; set fallback_to_random=true so collections without that field still get sampled.
Use rows_per_page to control sample size for RANDOM/LATEST and pagination batch size for ALL strategy.

detectors.title: Detectors
detectors.summary: MongoDB collections often contain PII, secrets, and sensitive business data — detectors are highly recommended.
suggestions: Enable SECRETS to catch API keys, tokens, and credentials stored in documents (common in config or session collections).
Enable PII to detect names, emails, phone numbers, and government IDs in user/customer collections.
Enable FINANCIAL to detect card numbers, IBANs, and account references in transaction or billing collections.
Run SECRETS + PII together for most Atlas deployments — they are fast, regex-based, and add minimal overhead.
Custom detectors (CLASSIFIER / ENTITY / RULESET) can be layered on top for domain-specific classification.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a workspace-oriented name so teams can quickly identify PowerBI scope.
suggestions: Include tenant or business domain (example: Finance PowerBI Tenant A).
If this is workspace-scoped, include the workspace name in the source name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: PowerBI requires an auth mode switch: SERVICE_PRINCIPAL uses tenant/client IDs, ACCESS_TOKEN uses a pre-issued bearer token.
suggestions: For SERVICE_PRINCIPAL, set required.tenant_id and required.client_id.
For ACCESS_TOKEN, set required.auth_mode=ACCESS_TOKEN and provide masked.access_token.
Keep all secrets in masked fields only.
questions: Do you want SERVICE_PRINCIPAL or ACCESS_TOKEN authentication?
Which tenant/workspace scope should be scanned?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Use masked.client_secret for service principal auth or masked.access_token for bearer-token auth.
suggestions: Prefer service principal auth for long-lived scheduled ingestion.
Use access tokens for short-lived delegated flows only.
Never embed secrets inside optional.connection URLs or extra options.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection tunes API behavior, optional.scope limits workspaces, and optional.extraction toggles entity groups.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.workspace_ids or workspace_names to reduce scan cost.
Set optional.extraction.extract_dataset_schema=true when dataset table metadata is required.
Set optional.extraction.extract_ownership=true only when principal permissions allow owner reads.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many PowerBI assets are extracted per run.
suggestions: Use RANDOM for broad coverage across dashboards/reports/datasets.
Use LATEST with order_by_column=modifiedDateTime when workspace APIs expose update timestamps.
Use ALL for full tenant/workspace inventories or baseline audits.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a Tableau scope-oriented name that reflects site and ownership context.
suggestions: Include the Tableau site and business domain (example: Tableau Finance).
If scoped to one project, include the project name in the source name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Tableau supports auth mode switching: USERNAME_PASSWORD or PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN.
suggestions: Set required.connect_uri and required.site for every Tableau source.
Use USERNAME_PASSWORD with masked.username and masked.password for server-managed credentials.
Use PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN with required.token_name and masked.token_value for Tableau Cloud automation.
questions: Do you want USERNAME_PASSWORD or PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN authentication?
Which Tableau connect_uri and site should be scanned?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Store Tableau credentials or token values only in masked fields.
suggestions: USERNAME_PASSWORD mode requires masked.username and masked.password.
PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN mode requires masked.token_value.
Never embed credentials in connect_uri or optional fields.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: optional.connection tunes API retries and TLS, optional.scope narrows projects/workbooks/datasources, and optional.extraction controls tags/owners/usage stats.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.project_names/workbook_names/datasource_names to reduce scan cost.
Enable optional.extraction.ingest_tags and ingest_owner only when downstream governance needs them.
Enable optional.extraction.extract_usage_stats only when Tableau permissions expose usage metadata.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many Tableau assets are extracted per run.
suggestions: Use RANDOM for broad workbook and datasource coverage.
Use LATEST with order_by_column=updated_at to prioritize recently changed Tableau assets.
Use ALL for full Tableau inventories when scope is small.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Use a Confluence instance and scope-oriented name so teams can identify coverage quickly.
suggestions: Include tenant and scope (example: Acme Confluence Engineering).
If multiple environments exist, include prod/staging in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Cloud ingestion requires base URL and account email for Basic auth with an API token.
suggestions: Use base tenant URL (example: https://your-domain.atlassian.net).
Use the Atlassian account email tied to your API token.
questions: What is the Confluence Cloud base URL?
Which account email should be used for API access?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Confluence Cloud uses API token authentication; token stays in masked config only.
suggestions: Create an Atlassian API token from id.atlassian.com and store it in masked.api_token.
Minimum read scopes for this connector: read:space:confluence, read:page:confluence, read:comment:confluence, read:attachment:confluence.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.connection, optional.scope.spaces, and optional.content to tune API behavior and extraction depth.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.spaces keys/ids/type/status/labels to constrain ingestion.
Enable comments and attachments for detector context and richer lineage.
Tune request_timeout_seconds and max_retries for network reliability.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many Confluence pages are processed per run.
suggestions: Use LATEST for frequent syncs focused on recently updated pages.
Use RANDOM for broad health checks across spaces.
Use ALL only when full historical scanning is required.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Name should reflect Jira tenant and project scope for easy ownership.
suggestions: Include tenant and domain area (example: Acme Jira Product).
If this is security-focused, include SEC/IR in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Cloud ingestion requires base URL and account email for Basic auth with an API token.
suggestions: Use base tenant URL (example: https://your-domain.atlassian.net).
Use the Atlassian account email tied to your API token.
questions: What is the Jira Cloud base URL?
Which account email should be used for API access?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Jira Cloud token stays in masked config; connector uses Basic auth with account email.
suggestions: Store only api_token in masked config; keep email in required.account_email.
Classic read scope: read:jira-work. Granular read scope guidance includes issue/comment/attachment/project read scopes.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.connection, optional.scope, and optional.content to tune Jira API behavior and extraction depth.
suggestions: Set optional.scope.project_keys/project_ids/jql when you want to limit extraction to specific teams or workflows.
Leave optional.scope empty to scan all visible issues, then control volume with sampling.
Tune include_comments/include_attachments for cost vs context.
Set max_retries and timeout settings for high-volume projects.
questions: Do you want to limit Jira ingestion to specific projects?
Do you have a JQL filter to narrow issue selection?

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many issues are processed each run from the selected (or full) Jira scope.
suggestions: Use LATEST for operational monitoring of recent ticket changes.
Use RANDOM for broad project quality checks.
Use ALL only for smaller tenants or periodic full audits.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Name should reflect Jira Service Management tenant and support scope for easy ownership.
suggestions: Include tenant and support domain (example: Acme Service Desk Support).
If focused on incidents, include Incident/IR in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Cloud ingestion requires base URL and account email for Basic auth with an API token.
suggestions: Use base tenant URL (example: https://your-domain.atlassian.net).
Use the Atlassian account email tied to your API token.
questions: What is the Jira Service Management base URL?
Which account email should be used for API access?

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Service Desk token stays in masked config; connector uses Basic auth with account email.
suggestions: Store only api_token in masked config; keep email in required.account_email.

Classic read scope: read:servicedesk-request. Granular guidance includes read:request:jira-service-management, read:request.comment:jira-service-management, read:request.attachment:jira-service-management, read:servicedesk:jira-service-management, and read:user:jira.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.connection, optional.scope, and optional.content to tune Service Desk API behavior and extraction depth.
suggestions: Set optional.scope.service_desk_ids/request_type_ids/request_status to focus on relevant queues and workflows.
Leave optional.scope empty to scan all visible requests, then control volume with sampling.
Tune include_comments/include_attachments for cost vs context.
Set max_retries and timeout settings for high-volume queues.
questions: Do you want to limit ingestion to specific service desks or request types?
Should comments and attachments be included in extraction?

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many service requests are processed each run from the selected (or full) scope.
suggestions: Use LATEST for operational monitoring of newly updated support requests.
Use RANDOM for broad quality checks across request queues.
Use ALL only for smaller tenants or periodic full audits.

name.title: Source name
name.summary: Name the source after the Notion workspace and scope so coverage is easy to identify.
suggestions: Include the workspace name (example: Acme Notion Product).
If scoped to specific databases, mention the scope in the name.

required.title: Required
required.summary: Notion has no required connection fields; authentication is the integration token in the masked section.
suggestions: Leave required empty ({}); all access is driven by the token and what is shared with the integration.

masked.title: Authentication
masked.summary: Notion uses a Bearer token (internal integration secret or OAuth access token); the token stays in masked config only.
suggestions: Create an internal integration at notion.so/my-integrations and copy its secret into masked.notion_token.
Share the pages and databases you want scanned with the integration — the API only returns shared content.
OAuth public-integration access tokens work identically; paste them into masked.notion_token.

optional.title: Optional
optional.summary: Use optional.connection, optional.scope, and optional.content to tune API behavior and extraction depth.
suggestions: Use optional.scope.page_ids / data_source_ids to constrain ingestion to specific content.
Enable comments and files for detector context and richer lineage.
Override notion_version only if you need a different Notion API version than 2025-09-03.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Controls how many Notion pages and data sources are processed per run.
suggestions: Use LATEST for frequent syncs focused on recently edited pages.
Use RANDOM for broad health checks across the workspace.
Use ALL only when full workspace scanning is required.
Enable enable_ocr to extract text from PDF and image files before detection.

schedule.title: Ingestion schedule
schedule.summary: Automate how often each source is scanned using a built-in cron scheduler.
suggestions: Choose a frequency preset from the Schedule card on the source configuration page.
Each preset picks a random time within a safe window to avoid all sources running simultaneously.
Use 'Reroll' to regenerate the random time within the same window.
For precise control, expand the 'Advanced' accordion and enter a custom 5-field cron expression.
Nightly (12 am–2 am) is best for web crawlers and large ingestion jobs.
Weekday mornings (6 am–9 am Mon–Fri) suit team-facing sources like Slack.
Disable the schedule to run sources manually only.

presets.nightly: Random minute, 12 am–1:59 am UTC daily
presets.daily: Random hour and minute, once per day
presets.weekday_morning: Random minute, 6 am–8:59 am UTC, Mon–Fri
presets.weekday_business: Random minute, 9 am–4:59 pm UTC, Mon–Fri
presets.weekly: Random minute, 12 am–1:59 am UTC, Sunday

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.summary: Sampling controls how much content is fetched from a source each run, and in what order.
suggestions: LATEST fetches the most recently modified items first — best for catching new or changed content.
RANDOM picks a random subset each run — useful for broad coverage without recency bias.
ALL scans every item with no limit — use only on small or static sources to avoid long runs.
The limit field controls how many items are fetched for LATEST and RANDOM strategies.
For tabular sources (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MSSQL, Oracle, Hive, Databricks, Snowflake), set order_by_column to a date column (e.g. updated_at) to improve LATEST accuracy.
For S3-compatible storage, Azure Blob Storage, and Google Cloud Storage, LATEST uses provider last-modified timestamps while RANDOM selects a deterministic random subset from listed objects.
For MongoDB, LATEST uses newest documents first (by _id ordering) and RANDOM uses document-level random sampling per collection.

For PowerBI, sampling applies to discovered workspaces/datasets/reports/dashboards: LATEST sorts by metadata timestamps when available, RANDOM samples assets uniformly, and ALL keeps full inventory coverage.
For Tableau, sampling applies to discovered workbooks and datasources: LATEST uses metadata timestamps (for example updated_at), RANDOM samples assets uniformly, and ALL keeps the full inventory.
For tabular sources, rows_per_page controls sample size for RANDOM/LATEST and pagination batch size for ALL strategy.

strategies.RANDOM: Pick a random subset of items each run. Provides broad, unbiased coverage.
strategies.LATEST: Sort by last-modified date and fetch the newest items first. Best for monitoring recent changes.
strategies.ALL: Fetch every item with no limit. Suitable only for small or static sources.

SECRETS.title: Secrets detection
SECRETS.summary: Find API keys, tokens, and credentials.
suggestions: Core Secrets preset is high-signal for common providers.
Broad Sweep increases coverage but may increase noise.
Raise confidence_threshold to reduce false positives.

PII.title: PII detection
suggestions: High Risk PII focuses on sensitive IDs and financial info.
Contact PII is useful for emails and phone numbers.
Use severity_threshold to tune alerting.

YARA.title: YARA detection
YARA.summary: Scan for malware and suspicious patterns.
suggestions: Baseline YARA is a safe starting point.
Strict YARA increases timeout for deeper scans.

BROKEN_LINKS.title: Broken links detection
BROKEN_LINKS.summary: Check that extracted links and images are reachable and not empty.
suggestions: Enable for URL-heavy pages where link integrity matters.
Findings are low severity by default and focus on availability.

CUSTOM.title: Custom detector
CUSTOM.summary: Build user-defined detections using ruleset, classifier, or entity extraction methods.
suggestions: Use RULESET for deterministic keyword/regex matching with transparent logic.
Use CLASSIFIER to start with zero-shot labels, then add examples for SetFit fine-tuning.
Use ENTITY to extract custom spans with GLiNER labels in multilingual text.
Set a stable custom_detector_key so findings and feedback stay linked across runs.

RULESET.title: Ruleset detector
RULESET.summary: Deterministic detection using regex patterns, keyword lists, and scoring thresholds. Fully transparent — no training required. Best for well-structured patterns like IBANs, VAT IDs, GDPR keywords, financial amounts, and compliance markers.
when_to_use: The signal is a known pattern (regex), a keyword list, or a co-occurrence rule.
You need immediate results — no training step required.
Examples: IBAN numbers, GDPR keyword density, 'Vertraulich' markers, financial amounts above a threshold.

config_shape.regex_rules: Array of { id (string, required), name (string, required), pattern (string, required), flags (string, e.g. 'i' for case-insensitive), severity ('critical'|'high'|'medium'|'low'|'info') }. additionalProperties:false — NO label, NO weight fields.
config_shape.keyword_rules: Array of { id (string, required), name (string, required), keywords (string[], required), case_sensitive (boolean, default false), severity }. additionalProperties:false — NO label, NO weight fields.
config_shape.confidence_threshold: Top-level float (0–1). Default 0.7. This controls reporting sensitivity. There is NO flag_threshold and NO weight scoring.
config_shape.important: additionalProperties is false on every rule object — any unknown field (label, weight, flag_threshold) causes a validation error.

tips: Start with keyword_rules — they are easier to audit than regex.
Use flags:'i' on regex_rules for case-insensitive matching instead of duplicating patterns.
Always include a true-negative test: run the ruleset on clean text to verify zero hits.

CLASSIFIER.title: Classifier detector
CLASSIFIER.summary: Assigns a label (and confidence score) to the full document or a passage. Starts zero-shot with label names alone — add labeled examples to fine-tune with SetFit. Best for tone, intent, compliance category, language, and risk classification.
when_to_use: You need to categorize documents by topic, tone, intent, or risk level.
The signal is semantic — not a fixed pattern.
You want to start without training data (zero-shot) and refine later.
Examples: financial advice vs. information vs. opinion, toxicity, language detection, spam, anonymity risk.

config_shape.labels: Array of OBJECTS { id (string, required), name (string, required), description (string, optional) }. NOT plain strings — additionalProperties:false.
config_shape.training_examples: Optional array of { text (string), label (string) } pairs — for SetFit fine-tuning with 8+ examples per label.
config_shape.hypothesis_template: Template for zero-shot NLI, e.g. 'This text contains {}.' The {} is replaced with the label name.
config_shape.important: labels must be objects with id+name, never plain strings like 'advice'.

examples.name: Financial Advice Classifier
examples.note: 'advice' = directive ('Sie sollten...', 'Kaufen Sie...'), 'information' = factual reporting, 'opinion' = hedged personal view

examples.name: Toxicity Classifier
examples.note: Start zero-shot; add borderline examples to push the decision boundary.

examples.name: Hate Speech Classifier
examples.note: Three-label setup routes borderline to human review instead of auto-removal.

tips: label id and name are the zero-shot signal — make id and name specific and self-explanatory.
Add at least 8–15 training_examples per label before triggering SetFit training.
Use three labels (hate/borderline/safe) instead of binary to avoid false positive auto-removal.
Set confidence_threshold to 0.6–0.7 for production to reduce borderline misclassifications.

ENTITY.title: Entity detector
ENTITY.summary: Extracts and labels named spans from text (NER). Uses GLiNER for multilingual zero-shot extraction. Best for PII, domain identifiers, legal entities, DACH-specific structured values. Returns precise character spans, not document-level labels.
when_to_use: You need to find and extract specific named spans: names, IBANs, phone numbers, addresses, dates.
The output should include the exact matched text and its position.
You need multilingual extraction across German, Austrian German, English.
Examples: PersonName, IBAN, VATId, CompanyName, Address, PhoneNumber, Email, Date.

config_shape.entity_labels: Flat array of plain strings — the entity type names GLiNER2 will extract. Field name is entity_labels, NOT labels.
config_shape.entity_descriptions: Optional object mapping entity label -> description for schema-driven extraction quality.
config_shape.important: The field is entity_labels (not labels, not entity.labels). It is a plain string array, not objects.

examples.name: DACH PII Entity Detector
examples.note: Covers the full DACH business contact surface.

examples.name: Person Name Entity Detector
examples.note: Handles Austrian/German titles: Dr., Mag., Ing., Mag.a — and Umlauts in surnames.

examples.name: Date Entity Detector
examples.note: Handles: 03.03.2024, 3.3.24, 03. März 2024 — all common DACH date formats.

tips: ALWAYS patch config.entity.entity_labels (not config.entity.labels — that field does not exist).
entity_labels is a plain string array: ['PersonName','IBAN'] — never objects.
Use descriptive English label names even for German content — GLiNER is multilingual.
Combine EntityDetector with a RulesetDetector for high-precision validation.
Test against placeholder data ('Mustermann', 'AT00 0000...') to verify false positive resistance.

key_naming.summary: The detector key is a stable slug that links all findings across runs. Choose it carefully — it cannot change without breaking historical data.
suggestions: Use snake_case with a method prefix: ruleset_gdpr_keywords, classifier_financial_advice, entity_dach_pii.
Include the scope or domain in the key: iban_detector, vat_id_entity, toxicity_classifier.
Keep keys lowercase and hyphen/underscore-separated — no spaces or special characters.
Once set and used in findings, treat the key as immutable.

RULESET: Verify each rule triggers individually on a crafted test document.
Verify combined rules exceed the threshold on a real positive example.
Check near-miss patterns do not cross the threshold (boundary test).
CLASSIFIER: Measure precision and recall per label on held-out examples.
Build a confusion matrix — especially for borderline vs. extreme labels.
Check that confidence scores are calibrated (0.9 should mean ~90% correct).
Test on documents from all expected languages if multilingual.
ENTITY: Measure span-level precision and recall (exact match and partial match separately).
Test on placeholder/synthetic data to confirm false positives are suppressed.
Verify Umlaut-containing names and Austrian/German title prefixes are handled correctly.
Test all date format variants and phone number dial formats.
